flashcards.front: Distinguish between Verification and Validation in software testing.
flashcards.back: Verification asks 'Are we building the product right?' (checking artifacts against specifications via reviews, inspections, static analysis). Validation asks 'Are we building the right product?' (checking the actual product against user needs via dynamic testing, user acceptance testing).

flashcards.front: What is ISO/IEC 25010, and list two quality characteristics it defines.
flashcards.back: ISO/IEC 25010 is an international standard for software product quality, replacing ISO/IEC 9126. It defines eight quality characteristics: functional suitability, performance efficiency, compatibility, usability, reliability, security, maintainability, and portability.

flashcards.front: Compare Black-Box and White-Box test techniques.
flashcards.back: Black-box testing focuses on inputs and outputs without knowledge of internal code (e.g., equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis). White-box testing examines internal logic and structure, using code coverage metrics like branch, path, and statement coverage.

flashcards.front: Define Defect Density and Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).
flashcards.back: Defect Density (DD) = number of defects / size of software (e.g., per thousand lines of code), measuring quality. MTBF = average time between failures, indicating reliability; higher MTBF means more reliable system.

Defect lifecycle: 1) Detect and log defect; 2) Assign to developer; 3) Fix and retest; 4) Verify fix; 5) Close if resolved, or reopen if rejected. States include New, Assigned, Open, Fixed, Verified, Closed, Reopened.

flashcards.front: Differentiate Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC).
flashcards.back: QA is process-oriented, preventing defects through process adherence (e.g., standards, reviews). QC is product-oriented, detecting defects through testing and inspection. QA ensures 'doing things right', QC ensures 'doing right things'.

flashcards.front: What is Static Testing, and how does it differ from Dynamic Testing?
flashcards.back: Static testing reviews documents and code without execution (e.g., walkthroughs, inspections, static analysis). Dynamic testing executes the software (e.g., unit, integration, system testing). Static testing finds defects early, reducing cost.

flashcards.front: List the components of Cost of Quality.
flashcards.back: Cost of Quality = prevention costs (training, process improvement) + appraisal costs (testing, inspections) + failure costs (internal failures before release, external failures after release). Reducing failure costs via prevention is key.
